title: Quick introduction to the OpenLand package
text: OpenLand open-source R package analysis land use cover (LUC) time series. includes support consistency check loading spatiotemporal raster data synthesized spatial plotting. Several LUC change (LUCC) metrics regular irregular time intervals can extracted visualized one- multistep sankey chord diagrams. complete intensity analysis according (Aldwaik Pontius 2012) implemented, including tools generation standardized multilevel output graphics.

title: Quick introduction to the OpenLand package
text: data extraction contingencyTable() saves multiple grid information tables next processing steps. function returns 5 objects: lulc_Multistep, lulc_Onestep, tb_legend, totalArea, totalInterval. first two objects contingency tables. first one (lulc_Multistep) takes account grid cells entire time series, whereas second (lulc_Onestep) calculates LUC transitions first last year series. third object (tb_legend) table containing category name associated pixel value respective color used plotting. Category values colors created randomly contingencyTable(). values must edited produce meaningful plot legends color schemes. fourth object (totalArea) table containing extension study area km2 pixel units. fifth table (totalInterval) stores range first (Yt=1) last year(YT) series. Fields, format, description labeling lulc_Multistep table created contingencyTable given following: lulc_Onestep table, Yt+1 terms replaced YT, T number time steps, .e., YT last year series. study area, contingencyTable(input_raster = SaoLourencoBasin, pixelresolution = 30) returns following outputs: {r SL_2002_2014 <- contingencyTable(input_raster = SaoLourencoBasin, pixelresolution = 30)

title: Quick introduction to the OpenLand package
text: Intensity Analysis (IA) quantitative method analyze LUC maps several time steps, using cross-tabulation matrices, matrix summarizes LUC change time interval. IA evaluates three levels deviation observed change intensity hypothesized uniform change intensity. Hereby, level details information given previous analysis level. First, interval level indicates size rate change varies across time intervals. Second, category level examines time interval size intensity gross losses gross gains category vary across categories time interval. Third, transition level determines category size intensity category’s transitions vary across categories available transition. level, method tests stationarity patterns across time intervals (Aldwaik Pontius 2012). Within OpenLand package, intensityAnalysis() function computes three levels analysis. requires object returned contingencyTable() function user predefines two LUC categories n m. Generally, n target category experienced relevant gains m category important losses. intensityAnalysis() function returns 6 objects: lulc_table, interval_lvl, category_lvlGain, category_lvlLoss, transition_lvlGain_n, transition_lvlLoss_m. , adopted object-oriented approach allows set specific methods plotting intensity objects. Specifically, used S4 class, requires formal definition classes methods (Chambers 2008). first object contingency table similar lulc_Multistep object unique difference columns replaced appropriate denominations according LUC legend. second object interval_lvl Interval object, third category_lvlGain fourth category_lvlLoss Category objects, whereas fifth transition_lvlGain_n sixth transition_lvlLoss_m Transition objects. Interval object contains one slot containing table interval level result (St U values). Category object contains three slots: first contains colors associated legend items name attributes, second slot contains table category level result (gain (Gtj) loss (Lti) values) third slot contains table storing results stationarity test. Transition object contains three slots: first contains color associated respective legend item defined name attribute, second slot contains table transition level result (gain n (Rtin Wtn) loss m (Qtmj Vtm) values). third slot contains table storing results stationarity test. Hereby, Aldwaik Pontius (2012) consider stationary case intensities time intervals reside one side uniform intensity, .e. always smaller larger uniform rate whole period.
code: testSL <- intensityAnalysis(   dataset = SL_2002_2014,   category_n = "Ap", category_m = "SG" )  # it returns a list with 6 objects names(testSL) #> [1] "lulc_table"           "interval_lvl"         "category_lvlGain"     #> [4] "category_lvlLoss"     "transition_lvlGain_n" "transition_lvlLoss_m"

title: Quick introduction to the OpenLand package
text: Two auxiliary functions allow users check consistency input gridded LUC time series, including extent, projection, cell resolution categories. summary_map() function returns number pixel category single raster layer, whereas summary_dir() lists spatial extension, spatial resolution, cartographic projection category range LUC maps. OpenLand enables furthermore spatial screening LUCC frequencies one series raster layers. acc_changes() returns LUC time series number times pixel changed analysed period, returning grid layer table percentages transition numbers study area. Plotting map tmap function: Accumulated changes pixels interval 2002 - 2014 four time points (2002, 2008, 2010, 2012, 2014)

title: Quantitative Analysis and Visualization of LUCC
text: Intensity Analysis (IA) quantitative method analyze LUC maps several time steps, using cross-tabulation matrices, matrix summarizes LUC change time interval. IA evaluates three levels deviation observed change intensity hypothesized uniform change intensity. Hereby, level details information given previous analysis level. First, interval level indicates size rate change varies across time intervals. Second, category level examines time interval size intensity gross losses gross gains category vary across categories time interval. Third, transition level determines category size intensity category’s transitions vary across categories available transition. level, method tests stationarity patterns across time intervals (Aldwaik Pontius 2012).

title: Quantitative Analysis and Visualization of LUCC
text: data extracted rasters contingencyTable() function returns multiple grid information tables next processing steps. Within OpenLand package, intensityAnalysis() function computes three levels analysis. requires object returned contingenceTable() function user predefines two LUC categories n m. Generally, n target category experienced relevant gains m category important losses. intensityAnalysis() function returns 6 objects: lulc_table, interval_lvl, category_lvlGain, category_lvlLoss, transition_lvlGain_n, transition_lvlLoss_m. , adopted object-oriented approach allows set specific methods plotting intensity objects. Specifically, used S4 class, requires formal definition classes methods (Chambers 2008).
code: my_test <- intensityAnalysis(   dataset = SL_2002_2014, # here the outcome from the `contingenceTable()` function   category_n = "Ap", category_m = "SG" )  # it returns a list with 6 objects names(my_test) #> [1] "lulc_table"           "interval_lvl"         "category_lvlGain"     #> [4] "category_lvlLoss"     "transition_lvlGain_n" "transition_lvlLoss_m"

title: Performs the intensity analysis based on cross-tabulation matrices of each time step — intensityAnalysis
text: dataset list. result object contingencyTable. category_n character. gaining category transition interest (n). category_m character. losing category transition interest (m). area_km2 logical. TRUE change computed km2, FALSE pixel counts.

title: Performs the intensity analysis based on cross-tabulation matrices of each time step — intensityAnalysis
text: Intensity object

title: Performs the intensity analysis based on cross-tabulation matrices of each time step — intensityAnalysis
text: IA includes three levels analysis LUC changes. Consecutive analysis levels detail hereby information given previous analysis level (Aldwaik Pontius, 2012, 2013). interval level examines size speed change  vary across time intervals. category level examines size intensity gross  losses gross gains category vary across categories time  interval. transition level examines size intensity  category’s transitions vary across categories available  transition. analysis level, method tests stationarity patterns across time intervals. function returns list 6 objects: lulc_table: tibble. Contingency table LUC transitions   analysed time steps, containing 6 columns: Period:  <fct>. Evaluated period transition format    year t - year t+1. : <fct>. category year t. : <fct>. category year t+1. km2: <dbl>. Area square kilometers transited     category .    category period. QtPixel: <int>. Number pixels transited .    category category period. Interval: <int>. Interval years evaluated period. lv1_tbl: Interval object containing   St U values. category_lvlGain: Category object  containing gain LUC category period (Gtj). category_lvlLoss: Category object  containing loss LUC category period (Lti). transition_lvlGain_n: Transition object  containing annualized rate gain category n (Rtin)  respective Uniform Intensity (Wtn). transition_lvlLoss_m: Transition object  containing annualized rate loss category m (Qtmj)  respective Uniform Intensity (Vtm).

title: Performs the intensity analysis based on cross-tabulation matrices of each time step — intensityAnalysis
text: Aldwaik, S. Z. Pontius, R. G. (2012) ‘Intensity analysis unify measurements size stationarity land changes interval, category, transition, Landscape Urban Planning. Elsevier B.V., 106(1), pp. 103–114. doi:10.1016/j.landurbplan.2012.02.010 . Aldwaik, S. Z. Pontius, R. G. (2013) ‘Map errors account deviations uniform intensity land change, International Journal Geographical Information Science. Taylor & Francis, 27(9), pp. 1717–1739. doi:10.1080/13658816.2013.787618 .
